text: The surgeon performs transrectal natural orifice specimen extraction following a sigmoidectomy with DII lymph node dissection. The tumor is visually identified on the sigmoid colon. Dissection begins on the aortic plane, isolating the inferior mesenteric artery, which is skeletonized while preserving the right autonomic nerves. The interfacial space is reached and dissected, isolating all branches of the inferior mesenteric artery. The inferior mesenteric vein is identified and cut. The first sigmoid artery and superior rectal artery are divided, and lymph node dissection is carried out. The proximal and distal lines of resection on the colon are prepared.

The surgeon ligates the colon with a wire ten centimeters below the tumor to facilitate rectal washout. The lumen is opened below the wire, and the anvil is inserted into the abdomen through the anus. The proximal colon lumen is opened, and the anvil is positioned for a side-to-end anastomosis. The proximal colon is resected using a linear stapler, and the distal line of resection is completed with a harmonic scalpel. A plastic sleeve is inserted through the rectum into the abdomen, and the specimen is extracted.

The surgeon closes the lumen of the distal line using a linear stapler. The rectal stump is prepared for a side-to-end anastomosis with a circular stapler. A bubble test is performed to check for leaks, and a drainage tube is installed into the pelvis. The video then shows the abdomen three months after the procedure, highlighting the healed surgical site.
